questions.description: What are the general principles of EU law and, using examples from the case-law, discuss how are they used by the Court…
questions.text: What are the general principles of EU law and, using examples from the case-law, discuss how are they used by the Court of Justice to protect individuals and to ensure that: (i) the European Union institutions: and (ii) the Member States when implementing EU law, act in accordance with the rule of law?

Portarlington Pumps Ltd. have been manufacturing a range of car-washing devices and accessories since 1.993. Approximately 35% of their total sales of the Power Washer 2000, that they manufacture in Ireland and which is by far their best-selling product, are sold to customers in Ireland. Almost all of the remaining 65% of sales of the Power Washer 2000 are sold to shops in other EU Member States. Most of these sales are in the UK, with smaller volumes sold in Germany, France; The Netherlands and Belgium. Concerned at the high volumes of waste in the public water supply, and the high costs associated with supplying this water in the first place, the Scottish Executive is in the process of introducing a (fictional) statutory instrument, the Water Services (Amendment) Regulations 2018, placing restrictions on the marketing of, amongst other things, power washers that consume in excess of 18 litres of water per minute. The Power Washer 2000 uses 20.5 litres of water per minute. These restrictions will include: (i) a ban on advertising these power washers; (ii) a ban on offering free car- washing accessories with the purchase of the power washers; and (iii) setting a maximum nozzle size of 14cm in diameter for all domestic power washers. The Power Washer 2000 has a basic nozzle size of 16cm in diameter. Portarlington Pumps Ltd. are very concerned about the introduction of this statutory instrument, due to come into force in October 2018. Research conducted on behalf of the Scottish Executive has shown that sales of the affected power washers, such as the Power Washer 2000, are likely to fall by around 20% from late 2018 onwards as a direct result of these new marketing restrictions that will be introduced by the Water Services (Amendment) Regulations 2018. Portarlington Pumps Ltd. have approached your firm, asking whether there is any legal basis on which they can raise an objection to, or challenge the 2018 Scottish Regulations. You have been asked to investigate whether there is any potential conflict between the Water Services (Amendment) Regulations 2018 and Article 34 TFEU. Prepare an opinion for the company outlining, by reference to relevant case-law, whether the restrictions set by the Scottish Water Services (Amendment) Regulations 2018 are potentially incompatible with Article 34 TFEU on measures equivalent to quantitative restrictions on imports. In the opinion, assume that application of Article 34 to Scotland will not be affected following the United Kingdom's anticipated departure from the European Union in March 2019.

questions.description: The (fictional) EU Films and Television Productions Protection Directive… Damages, Directive.
questions.text: The (fictional) EU Films and Television Productions Protection Directive 2013/112 introduced, amongst other things, an obligation, set out in Article 3 of the Directive,requiring EU Member States to adopt national laws prohibiting the illegal downloading of films from the internet by 31st May 2015. This provision of Directive 2013/112 was transposed into Irish law by the (also fictional) EU Downloading of Films and Television Programmes Regulations 2015, SI No 345/2015, which established that downloading films from the internet without making a payment for this (Regulation 5), or facilitating others in illegally downloading such films (Regulation 6), was an offence punishable, on summary conviction, by a fine of up to €5,000 and/or a term of imprisonment of up to eighteen months (Regulation 9). The Irish Regulations came into effect on 31 st October 2015. Liam Lynott launched an application in March 2015, which was shut down at the end of August 2016, that allowed anyone who used this 'app' to download and watch films on their mobile phones, without any charge. In January 2018, Liam was convicted in the District Court of facilitating others in the illegal downloading of films on all dates between 1st June 2015 and 27th August 2016, contrary to Regulation 6 of the 2015 Irish Regulations, and he was handed a suspended prison sentence of two months as well as being fined €3,000. Liam is now also being sued for damages by Prion Film Distributors Ltd, who are an Irish company based in Longford and who were supplying newly-released films, including many of those which were available on Liam's app, to cinemas throughout west Leinster and Connaught since February 2013. Prion have estimated that Liam's app cost their business lost profits of approximately €120,000 per annum. Liam has launched an appeal to the Circuit Court against the severity of the sentence imposed upon him by the District Court. He is also looking to defend himself in the action for damages that has been brought against him by Prion Film Distributors Ltd. in the High Court Firstly, advise Liam about whether the case-law of the Court of Justice on the direct effect of EU directives might help him in: (i) his appeal against the severity of the sentence imposed upon him; and (ii) defending himself against the claim being brought by Prion Film Distributors Ltd. Secondly, by reference to case-law of the Court of Justice, assess whether the State might also be liable for the payment of damages to Prion Film Distributors Ltd. for any losses that they have suffered as a result of Liam Lynott's actions and, if so, in respect of what period such a liability may arise.

questions.description: Has the seminal decision of the Court of Justice in Case 25/62 Plaumann v Commission continued to make it 'literally…
questions.text: Has the seminal decision of the Court of Justice in Case 25/62 Plaumann v Commission continued to make it 'literally impossible' for a non-privileged applicant to 'institute proceedings against an act' of the European Union under Article 263(4) TFEU? In your opinion, refer to relevant case-law, legislation and to attempts that may have been made (particularly by the amendment introduced by the Treaty of Lisbon), to make it easier for non-privileged applicants to use Article 263 TFEU to review the legality of acts of the EU institutions. Consider also how any such attempts have been interpreted by the Union judicature in its relevant case-law.

questions.description: Bradley is an Australian national who moved to live in France in January… Company.
questions.text: Bradley is an Australian national who moved to live in France in January 2014, in order to take up an offer of employment as an investment fund manager with Banque du Nord in Paris. A keen mountaineer, Bradley resigned from this post with the bank in April 2017 to start working in a temporary position as an employed hiking guide in the Massif Central. In February 2016 Bradley got married to one of his colleagues at the Banque du Nord, a Dutch national named Sophie. The marriage did not go well and the couple separated in early March 2017, soon after Sophie informed Bradley that she never wanted to have any children. Bradley moved out of their apartment in Paris but Sophie still lives there. Neither Bradley nor Sophie has yet filed for divorce. Bradley was initially granted a French residence permit in January 2014, which lasted for four years. It was due for renewal by January 27th 2018. However, his application for renewal of the residence permit was refused. Bradley is devastated by this refusal to renew his French residency permit as he really enjoys working as a hiking guide in France and believes. that this job has helped him to deal with the break-up of his marriage. He is even considering setting up his own hillwalking and mountaineering company, running adventure holidays in several mountainous locations across France. Bradley has approached you as a legal expert to ask you whether there are any European Union laws that he could use to challenge this refusal to renew his residency and working permit. His initial appeal to the local authorities in France was rejected. Referring particularly to relevant EU legislation and case-law of the Court of Justice, advise Bradley.

questions.description: Following a series of scandals surrounding the contamination of powdered… Directive.
questions.text: Following a series of scandals surrounding the contamination of powdered baby milk and powdered infant formula produced in France, China and Brazil, the Irish Government, through the Department of Health, wants to establish a (fictional) scheme for the sampling and analysing of all such products, regardless of whether they are manufactured in Ireland, in other EU Member States, or in any third country. The (fictional) EU Directive 2017/678 on powdered baby milk and follow-on infant formula allows EU Member States to carry out sampling and analysis of these products, but it does not oblige any Member State to do so. According to the Irish Government's proposal, the costs of operating this scheme will be met by the imposition of a 10% value added tax on all imported powdered baby milk and powdered infant formula. The products of Irish producers will not be taxed in this way, but will, instead, have to be certified regarding the sampling and analysis undertaken and detailing the results of these investigations. Irish producers will have to pay for these tests and the related certification themselves. It is estimated that this will add an additional production cost to the Irish producers Of powdered baby milk and powdered infant formula of between 8-12%. You have been approached to advise the Department Of Health about the compatibility of the proposed scheme with EU law. In your opinion, referring to relevant case law of the Court of Justice and having regard to the (fictional) EU Directive 20171678, consider the compatibility of the proposed scheme with the TFEU provisions on charges of equivalent effect to customs duties and internal taxation.

questions.description: Write a case-note on ANY THREE of the following eight judgments of the Court of Justice of the European Union, setting…
questions.text: Write a case-note on ANY THREE of the following eight judgments of the Court of Justice of the European Union, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the Court of Justice, and (c) how those issues were addressed by the Court of Justice in its judgement: (i) Case C-352/98, Laboratoires Pharmaceutiques Bergaderm SA and Goupil v Commission [2000] ECR 1-5291; (ii) Case 106/77, Amministrazione delle Finanze dello Stato v Simmenthal SpA [1978] ECR 629; (iii) Cases 21-24/72, International Fruit Company v Produktschap voor Groenten en Fruit [1972] ECR 1219; (iv) Case C-438/05, International Transport Workers Federation (ITF) and Finnish Seamen's Union (FSU) v Viking Line ABP and OÜ Viking Line Eesti [2007] ECR 1-779; (v) Case C-212/97, Centros Ltd v Erhvervs-og Selskabsstyrelsen [1999] ECR 11459; (vi) Cases C-501, 513, 515 and 519/06, GlaxoSmithKline Services Unlimited and others v Commission [2009] ECR 1-9291 ; (vii) Cases 6 and 7/73, Instituto Chemioterapico Italiano SpA and Commercial Solvents v Commission [1974] ECR 223; and (viii) Case C-413/06, Bertelsmann AG and Sony Corporation of America v Independent Music Publishers and Labels Association (IMPALA) [2008] ECR 1 4951.

Answer Part (a) OR Part (b). Do not attempt both parts. (a) Referring particularly to the relevant case-law of the Court of Justice and EU legislation, prepare a critical assessment of whether the law is now sufficiently well-developed in relation to the issue of sex equality in employment that the focus of the relevant EU institutions may safely now be shifted to dealing with other forms of discrimination, such as that which is based on religious belief, age, race, disability and/or sexual orientation. OR (b) By reference to relevant case-law of the Union judicature concerning Article 102 TFEU, analyse the extent to which a dominant undertaking may take steps to protect its own commercial position in the market on which it is dominant, or on a related market, if its position is threatened by a rival and/or new entrant. Could such steps or protective behaviour be acceptable if the effect were specifically to strengthen or extend the undertaking's dominant market position.

questions.description: You have been approached by a UK Parliament House of Lords Select Committee to draft an opinion on what disadvantages…
questions.text: You have been approached by a UK Parliament House of Lords Select Committee to draft an opinion on what disadvantages there may be for UK citizens if the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights is no longer a source of human rights law in the UK following Brexit. Draft this opinion, which should include a discussion on the key rights set out in the Charter and the decided case law of the CJEU on the application of the Charter.

questions.description: Article 4(2) of EU Directive 333/2017 (fictional) reduces maximum… Directive, Regulation.
questions.text: Article 4(2) of EU Directive 333/2017 (fictional) reduces maximum permitted chlorine levels in swimming pools to 3 parts per million. This maximum permitted level was previously set by EC Directive 456/2008 (fictional) at 5 parts per million. EU Directive 33/2017 was introduced to address concerns raised in a number of scientific studies about the links between the overuse of substances like chlorine and bleach and the increasing prevalence of asthma in children and young adults. The Directive was to be transposed into the national law of all EU Member States by31 March 2018. Ireland has not yet updated the Recreational Waters (Chemical and biological Substances) Regulations 2010 (fictional) to reflect the new lower permitted levels of chlorine in swimming pools. These 2010 Irish Regulations set the maximum permitted chlorine level at 5 parts per million. David, who is 24 years old, joined his local public sports centre in Howth in May 2018as he tried to get fit after several years of relative physical inactivity. He started his new fitness regime by going swimming five days per week at the sports centre, which is owned and managed by Howth Council. However, after six weeks of this he started to develop a painful skin condition and breathing difficulties which have left him unable to do any exercise. He is subsequently informed by his dermatologist that the skin condition has probably been caused by the chlorine in the swimming pool. His GP has also informed him that the swimming may also have caused his breathing difficulties. Tests have shown that the average daily chlorine level in the Howth Swimming Pool during the summer of 2018 was 4.7 parts per million. Referring to the relevant CJEU case law, advise David as to (i) whether he can rely on the provisions of EU Directive 333/2017 when bringing a claim for compensation before the national courts against Howth Council; [and]{.underline} (ii) what he will have to establish in order to succeed in any such claim.

questions.description: Make a critical assessment about whether you think that the way in which the principle of proportionality has been…
questions.text: Make a critical assessment about whether you think that the way in which the principle of proportionality has been applied by the CJEU in its deliberations in relevant decided case-law concerning Articles 34-36 TFEU has made it too difficult for EU Member States to have national measures in place that are designed to protect consumers and to protect human health.

questions.description: Lola, from Argentina, moved to Ireland in August 2017 to undertake… Advise Lola.
questions.text: Lola, from Argentina, moved to Ireland in August 2017 to undertake research for a PhD in Law at the University of Portarlington (fictional). Her boyfriend, Fred, who is French, has worked as a chef in a restaurant in Portarlington since 2014. The couple met while Fred was touring Argentina by bicycle in May 2017 and Lola moved into Fred's apartment in Portarlington as soon as she arrived in Ireland. Lola and Fred are not engaged to by married, but they have discussed the probability of getting married at some stage in the future. The registration fees for a PhD at the University of Portarlington are €12,200 per year for non-EU students. Lola paid for the first year of her registration through a combination of personal savings (€4,000) and a partial grant (€8,200). However, the grant was only for one year. Lola has applied to several funding bodies for financial support for years 2, 3, and 4 of her PhD studies, but all applications were unsuccessful. Her student visa only allows her to work on a part-time basis for up to 18 hours per week. She earns on average of €225 per week working as a kitchen porter in the same restaurant as Fred. Her rent alone is €105 per week, leaving her with little money for the remainder of her living costs and with no way to pay the university fees. Lola does still have personal savings of €4,400. Lola is almost certainly going to have to drop out of her PhD programme. She will also therefore have to return to Argentina as her residency visa is only valid for as long as she is registered for study at the University. She is not at all happy about this as she and Fred are very happy together, and she has really been enjoying studying at the University of Portarlington. She also recently discovered that she is pregnant. Fred is the father. Lola has come to you for advice on whether there may be any provisions of EU law, as interpreted in relevant CJEU case-law that could assist her. Ideally, she would really like to remain on the PhD programme, and she would be happy to work additional hours at the restaurant while she still can to help pay for this. Alternatively, at the very least she would like to remain living with Fred and their future child in Ireland, even if she cannot continue with her studies for now. Advise Lola.

questions.description: Considering both (i) the amendments that have been made to the former Article 230(4) EC by Article 263(4) TFEU with…
questions.text: Considering both (i) the amendments that have been made to the former Article 230(4) EC by Article 263(4) TFEU with regard to certain Union acts; and (ii) judicial attempts that have been made to reform the test for individual concern in particular, analyse critically whether it has become any easier in 2018 for non-privileged applicants to seek judicial review of EU acts than it was when Advocate General Jacobs delivered his opinion on Case C-50/00, UPA v Council [2002] ECR I-6677. Your answer should include an analysis of the case-law of the Union judicature interpreting article 263(4) TFEU.

questions.description: Sandra is a 38 year old mother of three who has lived all of her life in… Advise Sandra on whether the TFEU provisions…
questions.text: Sandra is a 38 year old mother of three who has lived all of her life in Letterkenny, County Donegal. When she was 31 years old she started to have abdominal pains, which got progressively worse. After suffering these pains for nearly 15 months, Sandra went to see her GP who referred her to the local hospital for tests. Five months later, these initial hospital tests were inconclusive, so she was referred to see a consultant in a hospital in Dublin. After a further four months of waiting for an appointment in Dublin, the consultant there discovered that Sandra has a rare kidney disease that will continue to get progressively worse. While the disease is rarely fatal, it does cause the patient to suffer from a lot of discomfort. Sandra describes the pain as being 'intolerable most of the time and very bad the rest of the time'. Ultimately, Sandra will need to have a kidney transplant but as her condition is not thought to be fatal, she has been informed that it is likely to be many years before she reaches the top of the kidney transplant list. There is an interim operation that can be carried out on Sandra to relieve her pain and discomfort. Sandra, who does not have private health insurance or a medical card, was put on a public hospital waiting list to have this operation in March 2014. She was informed in December 2017 that there was still a 2-3 year waiting list for this procedure as there was only one surgeon in Ireland who can carry it out. Sandra is fed up of waiting for this operation and her pain continues to get worse. It is badly affecting her family life and she has had to give up her job as a hotel manager. She therefore looked into other options and later arranged to have the operation done in Germany at the cost of €8,500. She borrowed nearly €5,000 from her sister to finance this operation and paid the remainder from the entirety of her own personal savings. The operation in Germany was a success. Upon returning to Ireland, a neighbour informed Sandra that she had heard someone being interviews on the radio about the possibility of getting reimbursed by the Irish Health Service Executive (HSE) for operations carried out abroad. This neighbour was a bit sketchy on the details, but she thinks that the person being interviewed on the radio said that such reimbursements might be possible in some circumstances under EU law. Sandra telephoned her local HSE office in Donegal but the person who she spoke with there stated that she was unaware of any such scheme for reimbursement and also that she would be surprised if what her neighbour told her was trues, especially if the operation could have been carried out in Ireland at a later date. Sandra has approached you, as a local solicitor, for advice. She is keen to know whether there are any provisions of EU Law that she might be able to use to make a case for reimbursement of the costs of her operation in Germany to the HSE. Referring to decided case lase of the CJEU, advise Sandra on whether the TFEU provisions on the freedom to provide and receive services could help her case.

questions.description: Write a case-note on ANY THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the…
questions.text: Write a case-note on ANY THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the CJEU, and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU, and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU in its judgement: (i) Case C-41/90, Hofner and Elser [1991] ECR I-1979; (ii) Joined Cases C-6/90 & 9/90, Francovich and others v Italy [1991] ECR I-5357; (iii) Case 6/64, Costa v ENEL [1964] ECR 585; (iv) Cases 21-24/72, International Fruit Company v Produktschap voor Groenten en Fruit [1972] ECR 1219; (v) Case 87/75, Bresciani v Amministrazione delle Finanze [1976] ECR 129; (vi) Case C-55/94, Gebhard v COAP di Milano [1995] ECR I-4165

questions.description: Launched a new type of hosepipe onto the Irish market in March 2018. Company.
questions.text: Grange Garden Products Ltd. Launched a new type of hosepipe onto the Irish market in March 2018. The hosepipe contains a device that was developed by grange Garden Products Ltd., the 4l-max, which limits the amount of water that can pass through the pipe to 4 litres per minute by pumping air into the water supply. Normal water consumption through standard hosepipes is 8-10 litres per minutes. During the drought of summer 2018, the Irish Government introduced a hosepipe ban in many areas to address low water supplies. However, this did not extend to those hosepipes which contained the 4l-max. Over 95% of all hosepipes sold in Ireland since July 2018 now use the 4l-max. Many other EU Governments introduced similar hosepipe bans during the summer of 2018, with similar exemptions for those using the 4l-max device. As a result, Grange Garden Products Ltd. Now has an 88% share of the EU-wide market in garden hosepipes, which were selling at wholesale for €24 in June 2018, but this has since risen to €31 per hosepipe. Each hosepipe that uses the 4l-max costs around €6.25 to make. The Irish Water Conservation Trust provided Grange Garden Products Ltd. With a grant of €1.75 million in February 2016 to research and develop the 4l-max. It has also recommended use of the 4l-max in its radio advertising campaign. The Irish Water Conservation Trust is a Government-funded body, the Board of which is appointed by the Minister for Communications, Climate Action and the Environment. Warwick water is a British company. It has seen sales of its hosepipes plummet during the summer of 2018. Although all hosepipe bans have now been lifted in those EU Member States where they were applied, Warwick water cannot get any of the large retailers in the UK or in Ireland to stock its products. It has been met with similar difficulties when trying to access markets in Germany and France. It subsequently discovers that Grange Garden Products Ltd. Has informed some retailers that it will no longer supply them with their 4l-max hosepipes if they continue to stock similar products made by anyone else, including Warwick Water. Fearing the reintroduction of hosepipe bans, many retailers across the EU have acted on this threat by only selling 4l-max hosepipes. Advise Warwick Water about any possible breaches of EU Competition Law and/or State Aid Law.
